Since we were traveling with a young baby, we planned to just explore the Famagusta-[Varosha](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Varosha+Famagusta+Cyprus) area rather than rushing around everywhere. For a 2-night, 3-day plan, it was more than sufficient, we didn't even have time to visit the [Salamis ancient city](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Salamis+Ruins+Famagusta+Cyprus).

E-SIM services that stopped working in Turkey still function in Northern Cyprus.

## Getting There

- The airport is spotless, exit is smooth, virtually no queues. In Turkey, they give you an additional form at departure; they forgot to give us ours, but interestingly it wasn't a problem at entry.
- Taxis are generally Mercedes. Airport to Famagusta cost 3,000 TL (about 70 USD). Reasonable for a 1-hour trip.
- Renting a car is actually the smartest option, but since traffic drives on the left, we didn't dare.
- Our pre-arranged taxi driver didn't show up for our return trip, making the airport transfer stressful. Don't try to save money with an unknown driver, stick with your hotel's recommendations.
- The airport lounge was quite mediocre. Not really worth entering.

## What to See

- If you're coming for the beach, late September is perfect, it's still warm. We went after closing beach season in Izmir, and the water was quite warm.
- The [Famagusta walled city](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Famagusta+Walled+City+North+Cyprus) is very enjoyable. It's a large, expansive area with historical sites that never seem to end. With some investment, it could easily outshine [Dubrovnik](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Dubrovnik+Croatia).
- [Lala Mustafa Pasha Mosque](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Lala+Mustafa+Pasha+Mosque+Famagusta+Cyprus) is truly impressive. Both massive and unique as a church-turned-mosque that's still actively used, quite rare.
- Entry to Varosha (the ghost city) is free. There's no beach entrance though, you need to go to the main gate. Buggy tours don't operate between 12-2 PM due to charging, and there are queues. Chauffeured rental is 750 TL for 3 people, 1,250 TL for 5.
- Varosha is a deeply affecting place. Currently only 20% is open, yet even that feels enormous. Seeing this environment makes your heart ache. It should be taught in schools worldwide from primary level onwards, a lesson for all of humanity.
- Late September is still very hot. If coming purely for cultural sightseeing, late October might be better.
- For the beach, [Palm Beach](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Palm+Beach+Famagusta+Cyprus) was superb. No sunbeds or equipment at the venue, but you can set your things on the chairs and get in. Safe and affordable. One of the best seas I've ever swum in.

## Food & Drink

- Don't go in with sky-high expectations for restaurants, but we were never unhappy with anything we ate either. Portions are especially generous.
- The halloumi cheese is excellent. We found a low-salt version at a place called [Koy Pazari](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Koy+Pazari+Famagusta+North+Cyprus).
- Don't say "what's Turkish coffee doing in Cyprus?" We really liked the Turkish coffee at [Oza](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Oza+Coffee+Famagusta+North+Cyprus). Much more aromatic than what we're used to.

## Tips & Advice

- The countryside is genuinely very barren, no trees, arid climate.
- Famagusta itself is very green. Water seems to be the key factor, with water available, it's almost tropical. We spotted Flamboyant trees at a few spots, the same ones we'd only seen in Cuba and Macau before, suggesting the climate would be tropical with sufficient water.
- Prices are very reasonable compared to Turkey. Large food portions. Safe and pleasant environment for exploring.
- Foreign tourists exist, especially Russians. Interestingly, there's a Black African working population.
- Buildings and structures are generally very old. Gave me a 1990s Turkey vibe. New large buildings are going up too, but like in Turkey, they're aesthetically bankrupt.

Finally, I don't usually recommend hotels unless something exceptional happens, but this time was different. The owner of [La Regina Veneziana Guest House](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=La+Regina+Veneziana+Guest+House+Famagusta+Cyprus), Tolga Bey, was one of the most hospitable and conversational hotel owners I've ever encountered. When we couldn't use our reservation due to the missing photo on the ID, he allowed us to use it at another time despite having no obligation to do so. He took great care of us during our stay. When our taxi driver ditched us for the airport run, he even drove us to the taxi stand in his own car. If you go, definitely stop by and have a chat. Warmest regards from us to him.

title: Rovaniemi
body:
## Overview

A small and cold Finnish city we traveled to in hopes of seeing the Northern Lights. They were visible from our area exactly one day before and one day after our stay, but not during it. Combined with our Iceland trip the previous year, this made it the second time we left empty-handed.

## Getting There

Since organized tour prices were outrageous, we went independently. Turkish Airlines has direct flights 2-3 times per week. We flew direct on the way there and routed through Helsinki on the return, both were comfortable.

Unlike Iceland, which is full of natural wonders, tourism here is built entirely around winter activities. We did, in order: ice karting, snowmobiling, horseback riding in the snow, a traditional Finnish sauna, and an ice hotel visit. All were genuinely fantastic.

## What to See

**Ice Karting:** We went to [Santa's Motor Park](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Santas+Motor+Park+Rovaniemi+Finland) across from [Santa Claus Village](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Santa+Claus+Village+Rovaniemi+Finland). Quite pricey, but drifting a car through the snow is priceless. Your hands will freeze, wear at least two layers of gloves.

**Snowmobiling:** Absolutely priceless. We joined Apukka's tour, riding across a frozen lake and through forests. They provide snow suits so you won't get cold, but bring sunglasses to protect from wind and glare. We loved seeing dedicated snowmobile road signs along the routes. It feels less like a tourist activity and more like using an everyday vehicle the locals actually rely on, which is exactly the case. One taxi driver told us he owns seven snowmobiles.

**Horseback Riding in Snow:** Also through Apukka. Since we had prior riding experience, we took the intermediate tour. Unfortunately, one of the four riders in our group fell off. No serious injury, but it scared everyone and cut the tour short. If you have no horse experience, you can skip this one.

**Traditional Finnish Sauna:** I'm not really a sauna person, my wife dragged me into this one. But I actually enjoyed it much more than regular saunas. The heater is essentially a wood-burning stove, like you'd find in Turkish villages, so the fire never gets boring. When it gets too hot, you step outside into the snow in a thin robe and come back feeling completely refreshed, able to stay much longer. I never imagined I wouldn't feel cold stepping out at -10 degrees in a bathrobe.

**Ice Hotel:** Since the largest ice hotel (Lapland Hotels Snow Village) is impossible to reach from Rovaniemi without a car, we visited the [Arctic Snow Hotel](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Arctic+SnowHotel+Rovaniemi+Finland), about 30 minutes away. More than sufficient, I'd say. The themed rooms and ice bar were great to explore. We didn't add the dinner package, and I'm glad we didn't, think twice before eating in a freezing ice restaurant.

**Husky and Reindeer Tours:** We chose not to do these after our pre-trip research indicated the animals aren't well treated. What we saw at the Husky Park near Santa Claus Village and the reindeer at our hotel Apukka confirmed this. I'd recommend skipping these activities if possible.

**Icebreaker Tour:** We had this booked but unfortunately had to cancel. Due to tragic news, we had to return a day early and missed our final-day activity. Hopefully we'll get to try it elsewhere someday.

**Santa Claus Village:** Seriously overrated. A 1-2 hour visit is more than enough and might even feel excessive. Without any connection to Santa culture, most of it feels empty. The line for a photo with the "official" Santa was insane. The Arctic Circle marker is a nice symbolic touch. For food, absolutely don't miss the [Salmon Place](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Salmon+Place+Santa+Claus+Village+Rovaniemi+Finland), they cook salmon over a wood fire, portions are huge. The place is tiny though, so expect a wait. No restroom on-site, so handle that beforehand.

## Where to Stay

Staying in Santa Claus Village is unnecessary. Unless you're going for the igloo hotel experience, a regular hotel in central Rovaniemi makes more sense, you can join Northern Lights hunting tours in the evenings. We stayed at Apukka, which had nice quality and atmosphere. Just a bit too aggressive with the upselling, they charge extra for everything.

If you're staying outside the city center, bring snacks and supplies. There are no stores outside Rovaniemi proper. In the center, [Shopping Center Rinteenkulma](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Rinteenkulma+Rovaniemi+Finland) has everything you need.

## Tips & Advice

The cold is real. Dress very warmly. Our record was -28C at 7:30 AM on our departure day.

On car rentals: we debated it since taxis are expensive and we wanted to visit the distant ice hotel, but the road conditions scared us off. If you don't have extensive winter driving experience, absolutely do not rent. Main roads are clear but everything else is pure ice. Even within the city center, you're walking on icy surfaces. At the airport, the runway was quite icy, our Turkish Airlines pilot took a good 15-20 minutes to taxi what should have been a very short distance. Finnish pilots are used to it and go full speed, but for us newcomers, caution is key.

The locals were all lovely. The small-town feel combined with regular tourist traffic makes everyone warm and friendly.

Not a huge draw for repeat visits, but if you've never done a winter activity vacation before, it's a great once-in-a-lifetime destination.

title: Paris
body:
We visited during the protests (July 3-6) but didn't actually see much unrest. Tourist areas are perfectly safe to explore. Police are everywhere after 7-8 PM, and since it stays light until 11 PM, you won't have any problems.

## Overview

My expectations were lowered by acquaintances who'd badmouthed the city, so the end result was that I was genuinely amazed. The tourist center is an absolute paradise. Every building is magnificent. Between the [Eiffel Tower](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Eiffel+Tower+Paris+France), the [Arc de Triomphe](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Arc+de+Triomphe+Paris+France), [Montmartre](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Montmartre+Paris+France), and the [Louvre](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Louvre+Museum+Paris+France), there were maybe one or two buildings that bothered my eyes. They've preserved their history impeccably. They absolutely deserve respect for that.

Paris is probably the most ethnically diverse European city I've visited. The African and Middle Eastern population is very visible, more so than anywhere else I've been in Europe. In Germany, the Turkish community has largely assimilated over generations, especially the younger ones who've essentially grown up as German-Turkish hybrids. Here the dynamic felt different. There's a very large immigrant population that still feels distinctly separate from French culture. I honestly wasn't expecting Paris to be this multicultural. It was the single most surprising thing about the city for me.

I'd heard endless complaints about filth and urine smells. Anyone complaining about the dirt should come see our country first. The urine smell didn't bother me nearly as much as in comparable cities. [Amsterdam](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Amsterdam+Netherlands), [New York City](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=New+York+City+USA), or any tourist center in Italy are far worse. Here it seemed more of a dog urine problem than human. The biggest issue was definitely the smoking. Everyone smokes like crazy. If you sit outside at any venue, prepare for constant smoke from all directions. Worse than Turkey, I'd say. Trash seems to get collected around 9-10 PM, so if you're out on the streets around that time, don't be surprised by overnight garbage piles.

## Food & Drink

The country is a gastronomy and wine paradise. We'd actually gone for wine education, and while I used to think they were a bit full of themselves, I was wrong. Their knowledge and expertise in this area is phenomenal. At any random cafe or restaurant, you can drink excellent wines by the glass for 4-5 euros. While Italian equivalents would be house wines made by the restaurants themselves, here they serve wines with AOC/AOP appellation certification in almost 99% of cases. And these appellation controls aren't limited to wine; you can see AOC/AOP labels on cheese and many other products.

Food-wise, we didn't get to try snails. The onion soups were decent (we'd had a much better one at a French restaurant in [San Francisco](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=San+Francisco+California+USA)). The duck legs were incredibly delicious, and the meats were good too. With a budget of 30-40 euros per person, you can enjoy very high-quality food-and-wine pairings. Even below that budget, the patisseries are absolutely phenomenal. The quiche at any random boulangerie patisserie you walk into will be superb. Our favorite quiche spot was [Sylvain Marie](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Sylvain+Marie+Boulangerie+Montmartre+Paris+France) near Montmartre. For restaurants, if you love truffle, definitely try [Truffesfolies](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Truffesfolies+Paris+France), and for a traditional French meal (duck legs), [Le Sens Unique](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Le+Sens+Unique+Paris+France). [Laduree](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Laduree+Paris+France) felt a bit overhyped at first but is definitely worth trying.

## What to See

The [Sacre-Coeur Basilica](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Sacre+Coeur+Basilica+Paris+France) and Montmartre area are absolutely beautiful. Climbing to the top of the basilica costs 7 euros and 290 steps. Quite tiring, but the panorama from the top is gorgeous, highly recommended.

[Notre Dame](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Notre+Dame+Cathedral+Paris+France) was still closed for interior visits at the time, but worth seeing from outside. The fire photo exhibition set up in front of it is quite moving.

I used to think the Eiffel Tower was a bit overhyped too, but seeing it up close genuinely leaves you in awe. Maybe it's from years of exposure, but it looked absolutely beautiful. The fact that it was built in 1889 is almost impossible to believe. The only slight letdown is that it was constructed for an exposition, so there's no deep history or story behind it. For the best Eiffel viewing spot, head to [Trocadero Gardens](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Trocadero+Gardens+Paris+France).

## Budget

If you're planning to properly explore museums and such, even 15 days wouldn't be enough for Paris. With its historic fabric, gastronomy, and everything else, it's a complete tourist paradise. After our visit, the fact that it's the world's most-visited city made a lot more sense. Definitely a destination I'll be planning to revisit.

title: Nuremberg
body:
## Overview

Despite the Christmas markets being closed, we still thoroughly enjoyed exploring this city. Compared to other German cities we'd visited, Nuremberg felt much more lively and fun, though that might have been the Christmas season effect. The historical fabric of the city is truly beautiful. Even getting lost in any random alley of the old town is a delight. Definitely worth more than one day, but three or more might be too much. A two-day visit seems ideal.

## What to See

The [Nuremberg Castle](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Nuremberg+Castle+Germany) is lovely, and following the city walls while exploring is genuinely enjoyable. We also visited the [Nazi Party Rally Grounds](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Reichsparteitagsgelande+Nuremberg+Germany) (Reichsparteitagsgelande), but we visited during COVID restrictions so we could only see the exterior. Even from outside, it's a historically significant structure worth visiting.

## Food & Drink

Restaurants were completely packed on Saturday, reservations are essential for a good dinner spot. The Gluhwein (hot mulled wine) is really excellent, especially while walking around in cold weather.

[Cafe Pique Nique](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Cafe+Pique+Nique+Nuremberg+Germany) has truly outstanding bagels, a must for breakfast or a snack. [Cafe & Bar Celona](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Cafe+%26+Bar+Celona+Nuremberg+Germany) is also a fun spot, particularly for weekend evenings when you can enjoy your meal or drinks with live music. Their tapas were solid.

## Tips & Advice

Hoping to return someday when the Christmas markets are actually open.

title: Athens
body:
## Overview

The capital of Greece, which I had the chance to visit for the second time as part of a cruise trip.

The city center is generally unattractive. There's no concept of building aesthetics, and everything looks dated. A coat of paint would do wonders. The situation is the same as in Turkey: when you combine bad taste with no money, this is what you get.

That said, the Greeks have an incredibly dominant cultural heritage. The word "Olympics" comes from the village of Olympia, and "marathon" comes from the town of Marathon.

[Ermou Street](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Ermou+Street+Athens+Greece) is their version of Istiklal Avenue, but aesthetically it's in the negatives. Seriously rough.

## What to See

The [Acropolis](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Acropolis+Athens+Greece) is truly magnificent, both in terms of its location and the structures within. An incredible place. During peak season the queues are brutal, so try to buy tickets a day in advance for your preferred time slot to avoid standing in line. It gets very crowded inside too, so morning visits are best.

## Getting There

If you're arriving by cruise, there's a metro connecting [Piraeus](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Piraeus+Port+Athens+Greece) to Athens, and the ride in is very easy. Just factor in an extra 30-40 minutes for the return, because the walk from the metro back to the ship at the port is quite long.

title: Greek Island Cruise
body:
## Overview

The name of the Celestyal Cruises ship we had the chance to cruise the Greek islands on for a week in September 2023.

Since this was our first cruise experience, I've compiled my thoughts on both cruising in general and this ship in particular.

The cruise concept that sounds so logical in theory turns out to be genuinely that logical in practice. Traveling between destinations overnight while you sleep, without wasting any daylight hours, is a brilliant setup. It saves you a ton of time. The big downside, though, is that you can only spend one day max at each stop, and the ports of call are usually places that deserve more than a single day.

## Food & Drink

Our package was all-inclusive for food, and the meals were genuinely delicious. This is great, but it creates an unexpected downside: since the onboard food is so good, you don't want to spend money eating out, which means you miss out on local flavors at the places you visit.

The buffet food was superb but the drinks they served alongside were mediocre. Beer and coffee were bad, and don't even get me started on the wine.

One catch: everything is all-inclusive except bottled water, which they charge for. Bring a large water bottle with you, you'll need it for drinking water in your cabin.

## What to See

The shows initially looked cheesy but they got better as the week went on. Every single performer was genuinely talented.

The extra excursions were all nonsensical. They start them at the crack of dawn and design them as 2-3 hour trips that get you back for lunch. I have no idea what the point is.

We had a complimentary bonus tour that came with our package, which we used for Athens. They gathered us at 7:15 AM, we waited around like idiots for ages, then they wrapped up the tour at 9 AM and said "okay, free time until noon," supposedly a 75-euro tour. If we'd actually paid for it, there would have been a scene.

## Tips & Advice

The ship was absolutely freezing because of the air conditioning. Fine, cool things down since it's hot outside, but set it to 22-23 degrees at least. They had it cranked down to 18 and we froze for an entire week. Definitely bring a cardigan on a cruise, even in summer.

People dress up ridiculously fancy for dinner. I hadn't even packed pants since it was a summer holiday. Without pants, you can't eat at any of the a la carte restaurants except the casual dining one. Packing one pair of pants and a couple of shirts is a smart move.

The cabins on Celestyal Journey were a bit dated but clean. There was a general smell issue in the ship and cabin bathrooms that would hit you from time to time. After a while, the dated vibe started to feel nostalgic, like traveling in the 1980s.

## Budget

On the return trip, we were thrilled to be arriving early, only to learn the ship has to enter port at its scheduled time or pay extra for early docking. So we spent the last 2 hours crawling along at a snail's pace. We would've gladly paid whatever it cost to get off earlier.

Overall, a cruise holiday is very enjoyable if you pick a good route. I was generally happy with Celestyal Journey and would definitely do it again.

title: Crete
body:
## Overview

Greece's largest island, which we got to visit as a day stop on a cruise. The island is genuinely huge with apparently plenty to see, but that's just not feasible on a single-day visit. [Heraklion](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Heraklion+Crete+Greece) has a few historical structures, but those take 2 hours max. We opted to visit two wineries about 20-30 minutes from the center and thoroughly enjoyed ourselves ([Lyrarakis Winery](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Lyrarakis+Winery+Crete+Greece) in particular was excellent). If that's not your thing, you'll need to find alternative activities, because Heraklion alone isn't very satisfying for pure tourism.

For more detailed wine notes, check our blog post on Greek Islands wineries at grapefultravelers.com.

title: Ipsala Border Crossing
body:
## Getting There

The [Ipsala Border Crossing](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Ipsala+Border+Crossing+Turkey+Greece) this year the crossings were truly terrible. You can get through the Turkish side quickly, but on both entry and exit, the Greek side has insane queues. Evening, weekday, nothing makes a difference. I can't even imagine the chaos during weekends and holidays. It honestly makes you not want to cross into Greece at all during summer. Next year we're definitely shifting our travel plans toward spring and autumn.

One observation: between 5 PM and 8 PM, there's only a single person working. This causes an incredible bottleneck. You either need to cross before 5 PM, or wait until very late. Otherwise, a queue that would normally take 30 minutes can suddenly balloon to 3-4 hours.

title: Milos
body:
## Overview

A Greek island we visited as a day stop on our cruise. The weather was so bad that we could barely even get off the boat due to the waves.

## What to See

[Sarakiniko Beach](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Sarakiniko+Beach+Milos+Greece) is an absolute must-see natural wonder. It feels like walking on the moon. Even if you can't swim, it's worth going just to explore. You really need to come in August though, we got absolutely battered by the wind in early September.

## Getting There

For taxis, there's an island-specific app called Milos App. Download it and book through there. Compared to other Greek islands, prices are more reasonable, the [Adamantas](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Adamantas+Milos+Greece) to Sarakiniko ride cost us 15 EUR. Public transport to the island's most popular cove runs only once every 2 hours, which is frankly ridiculous when you think about how easily they could increase the frequency to let everyone get around comfortably.

## Tips & Advice

There's none of the unnecessary tourist overcrowding you get at Mykonos and Santorini. For a beach holiday, this looks like the best route, but definitely come in August.

title: Mykonos
body:
## Overview

One of Greece's most popular islands, which we had the chance to see on a day trip via our cruise.

The town center is beautiful, tidy, well-kept, with a nice atmosphere. Just extremely windy. Overall more orderly than Santorini.

## What to See

We went to [Platis Gialos Beach](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Platis+Gialos+Beach+Mykonos+Greece). The sea looks truly gorgeous, but despite it being early September, the wind was incredible. The weather also turned overcast, so we unfortunately couldn't enjoy a proper beach day. Definitely best to visit in July or August. Sunbeds are 20 EUR per person. Some spots let you sit for free but their drinks are very expensive, and the types running those places are pretty shady, no need to sit there.

## Getting There

People always say there's a transport problem on the island with limited taxis, but we found both taxis and Uber available whenever we needed them. Just expensive. One-way rides generally cost 35-40 EUR. Definitely negotiate.

title: Rhodes
body:
## Overview

Despite everyone saying you need at least 2-3 days to do it justice, we only managed a day trip due to time constraints and visa reasons. Let me say upfront: [Rhodes](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Rhodes+Old+Town+Greece) is clearly a level above the other Greek islands. The Old Town is superbly enjoyable. I completely agree with those who recommend more time, but if the choice is between never seeing it and a day trip, I'd pick the day trip every time.

## What to See

The [Rhodes Castle](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Palace+of+the+Grand+Master+Rhodes+Greece) is a must-see, and the [Street of the Knights](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Street+of+the+Knights+Rhodes+Greece) should absolutely not be skipped. You can also walk the full perimeter of the castle walls from outside for beautiful views. Make sure to get lost wandering the Old Town streets.

The acropolis area has nothing worth the trip, but the viewpoints just behind it are absolutely stunning.

The new town part is pretty ugly. No need to explore it.

## Food & Drink

Seafood and fish are superb. We had swordfish at a restaurant called [Taverna Costas](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Taverna+Costas+Rhodes+Greece) and it was really enjoyable.

## Tips & Advice

The number of planes landing on the island is genuinely staggering. One lands every five minutes. I doubt [Izmir](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Izmir+Turkey) gets this much air traffic. It really shows the level of tourism these guys have achieved. Quite sad from our perspective. If only we could properly leverage the opportunities we have.

title: Chios
body:
## Overview

A Greek island we visited for a quick 2-day trip, just a 30-minute ferry ride from [Cesme](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Cesme+Izmir+Turkey). This was the second island I'd visited after [Rhodes](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Rhodes+Greece), and it fell quite short of Rhodes in terms of tourist appeal. Rhodes' Old Town and castle were spectacular. The city center here is pretty mediocre for sightseeing. The venues are quality, the seaside promenade is pleasant, the villages are ideal for day trips, and the sea is genuinely excellent and crystal clear, but cold. Two days feels more than sufficient.


## Where to Stay

We stayed at [Castro Rooms Chios](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Castro+Rooms+Chios+Greece) and it was genuinely great. Good value compared to alternatives. Recommended.


## What to See

- [Pyrgi](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Pyrgi+Chios+Greece) and [Mesta](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Mesta+Chios+Greece) are must-visit villages. You don't necessarily need to rent a car, there are day tours covering both. The tours are a bit rushed compared to driving yourself, but if you're short on time they work fine. If you do want to rent a car, absolutely book well in advance for high season. When I tried 2 weeks out, the next month was completely full. Book 1-2 months ahead.
- The [Chios Byzantine Museum](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Chios+Byzantine+Museum+Greece) is absolutely not worth entering. Save your 4 euros. You can peek inside for free from the courtyard edge. If you do go in accidentally, know that there's virtually no explanation in anything other than Greek.
- The castle interior is really run down, no restoration whatsoever. The only thing worth seeing inside is an Ottoman bath. You can also get a panoramic sea view from the spot marked "The North East Wall of Chios Castle" on Google Maps.


## Food & Drink

- The mastic tree is only harvested on this island, which is genuinely a unique fact. They've made everything imaginable from mastic, even mastic tea.
- If you like fresh beer, Chios Fresh Beer is quite good. I found the mastic version unnecessary, but the dark version is excellent, if you're a fan of the old Efes Dark, don't miss it.
- They have a coffee called fredo, made with espresso. Frappe, on the other hand, is made with instant coffee.
- [Bar 44](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Bar+44+Chios+Greece) in the center was a really fun evening spot. They have various Greek wines by the glass and good service.
- The bar called [Sueno](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Sueno+Bar+Chios+Greece) also had great breakfast, the omelet and fried egg were excellent. Most venues here operate from 7 AM to 4 AM and serve everything. The seaside strip venues are generally high quality.


## Tips & Advice

- [Karfas Beach](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Karfas+Beach+Chios+Greece) is normally cold but felt warm since the weather was scorching hot. The shallow section stays warm. Perfect for families with kids, it doesn't deepen at all. Crystal clear water. For reference: a beer is 5 euros, a soda is 3.50 euros, no separate sunbed charge, just pay for what you consume.
- On day two we went to [Glaroi Beach](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Glaroi+Beach+Chios+Greece), which is mostly locals. Unlike Karfas, sunbed charge here is 2 euros (comes with a water). Same drink prices. The food is bad. But the sea is really beautiful. Unlike Karfas, it deepens quickly, so it's a bit colder. The music and vibe are young and fun.

Being so close to Cesme, it could warrant another visit. But if it were further away, one visit would probably be plenty. Maybe they'll accelerate the restoration work and make it more appealing for tourists over time.

title: Samos
body:
## Overview

A Greek island we visited for 2 days and 1 night in July. The island is a decent size with quite a lot to see, making it a great location for planning a longer trip.

If you're thinking of it purely as a beach holiday, summer months make sense, but for sightseeing it's a terrible idea because it's scorching hot. The heat was so bad we didn't dare visit any of the historical sites.

## Where to Stay

[Pythagoreion](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Pythagoreion+Samos+Greece) struck me as the most sensible place to stay. [Kokkari](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Kokkari+Samos+Greece) isn't bad either but it's smaller. You can do day trips to the beaches from either.

## Food & Drink

Portions have shrunk noticeably compared to classic Greek standards, which was a bit of a letdown. Probably picked up bad habits from being so close to Turkey.

## Tips & Advice

The island is practically under Turkish occupation. You'll hear Turkish far more than Greek or English. But overall they like Turks here, the negativity you encounter in some other parts of Greece is completely absent on Samos.

title: Santorini
body:
## Overview

One of the most beautiful tourist destinations I've seen in recent years. It really hasn't become this famous for nothing. We had the chance to visit on a day trip as part of our cruise. The moment I saw it from the top deck after we docked, I knew we were somewhere extraordinary. The views are absolutely stunning.

## Getting There

But here's the thing, arriving by sea is an absolute ordeal. First, if you haven't booked an extra tour, cruise ships drop you at the entry point to [Fira](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Fira+Santorini+Greece). When we arrived, there were at least 1,000 people queuing for the cable car. The cable car takes 36 people per trip (6 cabins of 6) and a one-way ride takes about 5 minutes. You do the math on the waiting time.

So we decided, against all warnings, to take the stairs instead of waiting. The stairs themselves are very doable, but that's not the real problem. It's 2023, and they're still using donkeys. The stairs are literally covered in dung. An absolutely disgraceful situation, that the entry to Greece's most popular island is this, is just incomprehensible. Put in five more cable cars so tourists aren't humiliated. Or build a couple more exit points and move the donkeys or the people. I've never seen anything this absurd in my life.

The island's roads are generally quite rough; renting a car could also be a hassle. We used taxis throughout. A bit pricey, usually 30-40 EUR one way, but since we only used them three times all day, it wasn't too bad. Uber works on the island but sometimes quoted higher than taxis, so check both.

## What to See

The wine region of Pyrgos was very enjoyable. I'd recommend [Santo Wines](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Santo+Wines+Pyrgos+Santorini+Greece) for the view and [Boutari Winery](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Boutari+Winery+Santorini+Greece) for the quality. Walking between the two also treats you to magnificent scenery. Fira was decent but a bit run-down. [Oia](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Oia+Santorini+Greece), on the other hand, is genuinely beautiful. The sunsets draw enormous crowds, your best bet is to grab a seat at one of the sunset-view restaurants rather than fighting through the free viewpoints. Oia was probably the most crowded place I've seen after Alacati. But the atmosphere is absolutely wonderful. We were quite undecided about going before the trip, but afterward we could only say we're so glad we did.

## Tips & Advice

At 8:30 PM the cable car queue was completely empty. Once all the cruise ships leave, peace is restored. Let this be a recorded tip for anyone doing a day trip like us.

A place that absolutely belongs on any bucket list.

title: Thessaloniki
body:
A city we went to with high expectations but didn't quite find what we were looking for. First, the streets are genuinely filthy. It's astonishing. [Athens](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Athens+Greece) was far cleaner by comparison.

## Food & Drink

Second, we tried [Palati](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Palati+Restaurant+Thessaloniki+Greece), one of the highest-rated restaurants for the taverna experience. The live music was decent enough, but the food was honestly not very good. In general, seafood across Greece was both much cheaper and much better than in Turkey. That price advantage is still somewhat comparable despite the exchange rate shock, but the quality here was lacking. It might have just been an off night for us.

## What to See

My biggest disappointment was honestly the [Ataturk Museum](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Ataturk+Museum+Thessaloniki+Greece). On an emotional and spiritual level, it's an incredibly moving place, nothing to say about that. But as a museum, the content is very weak. Shoes displayed as Ataturk's own have 1990 labels inside them. Mistakes like this are unbecoming of such an important site. Parking around the museum is an absolute nightmare. You need to start looking for spots much farther away.

## Tips & Advice

It might make sense as a stop on the way to other destinations, but if you come specifically just to visit [Thessaloniki](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Thessaloniki+Greece), you may leave unsatisfied. Plan accordingly.

---

Greece's second-largest city, which I had the chance to visit for the second time as part of a cruise trip.

The cruise port is absolutely terrible. No signage, no proper building to speak of. You step off and just stand there like a lost chicken. For such a busy port, having facilities this bad is a joke. You have to walk aimlessly for about 30 minutes to find the exit, you'd think they could at least put in a shortcut.

## Getting There

For a day trip, the hop-on hop-off bus seems like a reasonable option. We hadn't visited the [Ano Poli](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Ano+Poli+Thessaloniki+Greece) area on our previous trip since it's farther from the center, and it turns out that side is lovely too. The [Yedikule Fortress](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Heptapyrgion+Thessaloniki+Greece) is old but nice to see from the outside, and the city views from up there are great. However, the hop-on hop-off narration and routes were both awful. Only get it if you need it purely as public transport; the guided commentary is nonexistent.

## What to See

- [Agios Dimitrios Church](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Agios+Dimitrios+Church+Thessaloniki+Greece) and the surrounding historic structures are beautiful. Thessaloniki is a genuinely ancient city with an abundance of historical sites to explore.

title: Budapest
body:
## Overview

A city we revisited during the 2023 Sziget Festival. Since we'd already covered most of the tourist sights on our first visit, this time we could explore without the pressure of missing anything, which made it much more enjoyable. We also got to properly enjoy the festival. If you're coming for Sziget and it's also your first time in Budapest, I'd recommend adding at least 1-2 extra nights beyond your plan to avoid cramming sightseeing.

## Food & Drink

If you're into wine tasting, this city is absolutely your kind of place. There were more wine bars here than we'd encountered even in Paris. Great flavors, very reasonable prices. My top three recommendations: [Champion Wine Vinarium & Gastro Boutique](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Champion+Wine+Vinarium+%26+Gastro+Boutique+Budapest+Hungary), [Tasting Table Budapest Shop](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Tasting+Table+Budapest+Hungary), and [Tokaj Wine Bar](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Tokaj+Wine+Bar+Budapest+Hungary). The first one was especially phenomenal, incredible quality wines for the price we paid. Whatever you do, don't leave without trying a proper Tokaji Aszu.

## Nightlife

For nightlife, [Szimpla Kert](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Szimpla+Kert+Budapest+Hungary) and [Instant](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Instant+Fogas+Budapest+Hungary) are the most popular spots. We walked into Szimpla Kert casually around 7 PM. Left to eat dinner since they don't serve food, came back around 9 and waited 15-20 minutes to get in. By the time we left at 10:30-11, they weren't letting anyone in and the queue was 3-4 times longer than what we'd waited in. Bottom line: don't leave your visit too late. Same story at Instant, we walked right in at 11 PM when it was partially empty, but by midnight it was packed and by 1 AM the queue outside was matching Szimpla's. Going a bit earlier lets you enjoy both far more.

## Where to Stay

We stayed at the [Ikonik Parlament Hotel](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Ikonik+Parlament+Hotel+Budapest+Hungary) and were happy with everything. Recommended.

title: Iceland
body:
## Overview

A fantastic country that left us absolutely spellbound during our one-week trip, we started planning a return visit before the trip was even over. It absolutely does not belong to this world. Photos and videos cannot do it justice. One of those places every human being must see before they die.

We went in March hoping to catch the Northern Lights, but the weather was too overcast during our week so we missed them. The harsh weather conditions also meant we couldn't fully enjoy the natural sites. The only upside of going in winter was experiencing the brutal conditions firsthand, we saw Iceland like a true Icelander. Beyond that, to fully enjoy the natural beauty, going in summer makes much more sense. For Northern Lights specifically, the weather here is too unpredictable, trying Norway or similar destinations would be smarter.

If you can manage it, the ideal plan is a summer trip of at least 2 weeks, doing a full loop around the island.

## What to See

- [Kerid Crater](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Kerid+Crater+Iceland): A crater lake. We saw it frozen in winter, absolutely mesmerizing.
- [Gljufrabui](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Gljufrabui+Waterfall+Iceland): A waterfall you can actually walk inside. Bring a full-body raincoat and spare shoes and t-shirt.
- [Skogafoss](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Skogafoss+Iceland): Climbing to the top is absolutely worth it, phenomenal panorama.
- [Diamond Beach](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Diamond+Beach+Iceland): Words fail. Utterly magnificent. Set aside at least 2-3 hours to fully soak it in.
- [Svinafellsjokull Glacier](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Svinafellsjokull+Iceland): We joined a half-day glacier climbing tour and it was the most impactful thing we did in Iceland. Next time we're doing the full-day tours. Can be combined with ice cave visits.
- [Dyrholaey Lighthouse](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Dyrholaey+Lighthouse+Iceland): The scenery and atmosphere here are also magnificent. Worth a stop.
- [Solheimajokull](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Solheimajokull+Iceland): Incredibly windy in winter, but summer conditions might be milder. The kind of place you could sit and watch for hours.
- [Reynisfjara Beach](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Reynisfjara+Beach+Iceland): I thought photos overhyped it, but seeing it in person was absolutely enchanting. Incredibly mystical atmosphere. Watch out for the waves though.
- Icelandic Lava Show: We expected a cheesy tourist trap and went in with low expectations, turned out to be a highly informative, genuinely scientific experience. Seeing real lava is an incredible experience.
- [Hallgrimskirkja](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Hallgrimskirkja+Reykjavik+Iceland): Absolutely one of the most impressive cathedrals I've ever seen. Simple, understated, yet utterly captivating.

## Food & Drink

- [Le Kock](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Le+Kock+Reykjavik+Iceland): A solid burger joint in central Reykjavik.
- [Forrettabarinn](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Forrettabarinn+Reykjavik+Iceland): Everything we ate was excellent, a great dining spot in Reykjavik.
- [101 Bistro](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=101+Bistro+Reykjavik+Iceland): Their fish and chips were superb. The owner is Turkish, say hello if you stop by.
- [Fosshotel Glacier Lagoon](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Fosshotel+Glacier+Lagoon+Iceland): We ate at this hotel while exploring the southern part. Again, everything was great.

## Tips & Advice

Especially if going in winter, bring plenty of spares for everything. You will get wet on many days, and walking around in wet shoes and socks in freezing cold is not something you want.

A fisherman's raincoat we bought from Decathlon for 60 lira, which we almost didn't buy because it looked a bit cheap, literally saved our lives. While many people's much more expensive Iceland-purchased raincoats were ripping apart, ours held up great and kept us warm. Usable even in summer for waterfalls and sudden rain.

title: Milan
body:
## Food & Drink

At [Yguana Cafe](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Yguana+Cafe+Milan+Italy), about 15 minutes from the [Duomo](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Duomo+di+Milano+Milan+Italy), you can buy a cocktail or drink for 13 euros and then eat unlimited from the buffet all night long. In Italy they call this concept aperitivo. While different cities have different versions, my best experience with it has definitely been at this place. We'd been there several times years ago, and on our most recent visit we went again, and the food quality hasn't dropped at all. Incredible value for money. If I'm not mistaken, the buffet opens after 7 PM.

## What to See

The Duomo is one of the most impressive man-made structures in Europe. Along with [La Sagrada Familia](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=La+Sagrada+Familia+Barcelona+Spain), I'm blown away all over again by both its sheer scale and its intricate details every time I visit. Worth making multiple trips to see it in both daylight and at night.

title: Venice
body:
## Overview

An absolutely magnificent city. Even on a 10th visit, you'd never get bored. Every street is original, with small shops, boutiques, and centuries-old details waiting to be discovered around every corner. Walking around genuinely feels like being in the 1800s. On my last visit, I wanted to move there for a month and walk every single street until I'd memorized the whole place, that's how impeccably beautiful this city is. Instead of wasting time in Italy's relatively less impressive cities, I'd strongly recommend extending your stay in Venice.

## Food & Drink

[Rossopomodoro](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Rossopomodoro+Venice+Italy)'s pizza and [Dal Moro's](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Dal+Moro%27s+Fresh+Pasta+Venice+Italy) pasta are both highly recommended. On my latest visit, Dal Moro's portions had shrunk a bit and the flavor had slipped slightly compared to before, but it's still a solid value-for-money option.

## Tips & Advice

If a gondola ride isn't in your budget (or you'd rather skip the full experience), there are shared gondolas between [Basilica di Santa Maria della Salute](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Basilica+di+Santa+Maria+della+Salute+Venice+Italy) and [San Marco Square](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Piazza+San+Marco+Venice+Italy) for just 2.50 euros per person. It's better than never having ridden one at all, so worth including in your itinerary.

title: Verona
body:
## Overview

I definitely can't say this city isn't beautiful, but compared to Italy's other cities and considering its level of popularity, I find it a bit overrated. If I were starting an Italy trip from scratch today, I could easily name ten cities I'd visit before Verona. The popularity seems to be driven especially by American tourists, largely thanks to Romeo and Juliet.

## Tips & Advice

If you're planning an Italy itinerary and wondering where to go, sure, you can include Verona, but personally, I'd much rather spend an extra day or two in Rome or Venice. Or I'd go out of my way to see San Marino or take a stroll through Siena over visiting Verona any day.

title: Amsterdam
body:
## Overview

A city with a wonderful old town. I just completed my third visit, and all three times I thoroughly enjoyed exploring. No matter how much you've seen, coming back after a long time still makes for a delightful experience.

Alcohol is freely available, weed is legal, yet there's remarkably little unruliness. Everyone minds their own business. Despite being extremely touristy and packed with visitors, the city's size somehow absorbs it all. The historic character is also very enjoyable. Even without entering a single museum, just wandering aimlessly along the canals is more than enough.

## Nightlife

This time, we timed our visit to coincide with King's Day. King's Night, held the evening before, is said to be even more fun than King's Day itself, but this year it was calmer. Apart from some action in the Jordaan district, the city was quiet. King's Day, however, was incredibly crowded. Despite the massive turnout, the city's size handled it well. There was minimal congestion aside from a few bridge crossings. We couldn't get into the concert area in the Jordaan district this time because of the crowds. The whole city had a genuine festival atmosphere. I can honestly say I enjoyed it far more than an Oktoberfest vibe. Strongly recommended for festival lovers.

title: Delft
body:
## Overview

A pleasant little Dutch city. Worth a tourist stop if you're passing through the area, though it might be too small to fill an entire day. Best combined with other nearby destinations.

It has a real university-town vibe. It reminded us of [Eskisehir](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Eskisehir+Turkey) in Turkey. From our brief observations, [Delft](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Delft+Netherlands) would easily be a place we'd consider living.

title: Keukenhof
body:
## Overview

[Keukenhof](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Keukenhof+Gardens+Lisse+Netherlands) is a beautiful place. It's a bit overly touristy, but you can see an incredible variety of tulips and plants. Definitely enjoyable to explore, though the 20-euro entrance fee feels a bit excessive.

The park is large but handles the crowds well. Overall comfort is good, though the lines for ice cream and cafes get unnecessarily long.

## Tips & Advice

- Online tickets are cheaper, you can tell because nobody buys from the counter anymore. Parking is included automatically when you add it to your online cart; there's no ticket check at the lot. Entry times are printed on the ticket, but nobody actually checks whether your time slot has arrived; you can just walk in.
- During our late April visit, some tulips had already started being cut. Visiting a bit earlier in the season might be better.
- You can totally bring beer, wine, etc. and just sit and enjoy the scenery at your leisure. There were actually a few groups going full-on picnic mode as if they were at a local park back home. Thankfully nobody tried to fire up a barbecue, or maybe they have in the past.

title: Netherlands
body:
## Overview

Generally a country of tolerant, respectful, and polite people. There's a slight streak of frugality, but they're also surprisingly generous. On the train to King's Day celebrations, I asked someone if I could buy a beer, they handed me one and refused payment. When I insisted and gave them 5 euros for two beers, they naturally pocketed it. But the initial gesture of not asking for money was genuinely kind.

The houses are universally gorgeous. Even the most basic small town has a historical and charming character. Take any random Dutch village and transplant it to Turkey and it would cause a domestic tourism explosion.

## Getting There

The roads are peculiar, speed limits are too low, on/off ramps are very sharp, and people take them at high speed. Cyclists have excessive right-of-way, which has made them virtually oblivious to traffic. Shared lanes are a serious headache for drivers.

## Tips & Advice

The weather is genuinely unpredictable. During our week-long trip in late April, we had days where we sweated in t-shirts and days where we froze in boots, coats, sweaters, and scarves. When the sun is out, it gets incredibly hot, 25 degrees here feels nothing like 25 degrees in Turkey.

Most homes have only one toilet, usually separate from the bathroom. The sinks in the toilets are bizarrely tiny.

The fact that everyone has similar purchasing power is admirable. Even in the most rural areas, houses are beautiful. While our villages are poor and dilapidated, theirs are gorgeous by comparison.

Traffic lights have sensors, as someone approaches, they switch to green, especially in the evening. A brilliantly implemented piece of technology.

Toilets are paid even in shopping malls, with absurdly long queues.

Renovation in houses is allowed between 7 AM and 9 PM. People start drilling at 7:30. Unbelievable.

Practical problem-solving is weak. One driver had an accident at exactly the spot where a road goes from one lane to two, left the car right there, and caused a 2-hour backup. Five more meters and the traffic would have cleared. In Turkey, someone would have told them to move in 5 minutes, but here everyone just drove past silently.

While I really enjoyed Amsterdam as a tourist destination, certain aspects of their daily life left me somewhat disappointed.

title: Volendam
body:
## Overview

A very enjoyable Dutch village. It has a much more natural character compared to nearby [Zaanse Schans](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Zaanse+Schans+Netherlands), making for a more pleasant experience. It's semi-touristy, but as you move a bit away from the town center, you'll encounter locals going about normal daily life. Suitable for a day trip, though spending a night here would let you better soak in its storybook atmosphere.

title: Zaanse Schans
body:
## Overview

An overly touristy village, quite overrated.

The parking lot at the main museum is there but incredibly crowded and inadequate. Despite having capacity for at least 1,000 cars, the museum itself has only 2 toilets, accessible only by card, creating absurd queues.

Reminiscent of those artificially constructed "local villages" you see in Africa, a place that may have once been more authentic but has, by 2024, been completely transformed into a purely commercial tourist attraction.

title: Wrocław
body:
## Overview

Price-wise, Poland is much more affordable than Germany, especially in Wroclaw. I really loved the city's size. There's none of that Berlin-level chaos, but it's also not so small that you'd get bored. There are tons of tourist activities. We stayed for 2 nights, but even 3-4 nights wouldn't get boring. I think it would even be a great city to live in, cold winters aside.


## What to See

Following the dwarves around while exploring the city is genuinely so much fun. It gives you extra motivation to explore, a unique concept, and since each one is different and original, you end up with a great photo series.

My favorite attraction was definitely the [National Museum](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=National+Museum+Wroclaw+Poland). The ivy-covered building is absolutely stunning. Back home, everyone who sees even a bit of ivy creeping up our house keeps telling us it'll damage the walls and we should tear it off. I need to print out a photo of this building as the ultimate counterargument.

[Pierogarnia](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Pierogarnia+Wroclaw+Poland) is an original local specialty. We had the mixed plate at [Rynek 26](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Rynek+26+Wroclaw+Poland) and found it excellent value for money. Despite being a tourist spot, they haven't compromised on quality. For traditional vodka shots and the classic Polish bar experience, [Prewar](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Prewar+Bar+Wroclaw+Poland) is both cheap and perfectly fits the vibe.


## Budget

Significantly cheaper than Germany. Excellent value for food and drinks.

title: Lisbon
body:
## Overview

A city that is almost an exact copy of Istanbul. From the moment we landed until we moved on to Porto, we kept catching ourselves saying "wait, are we in Istanbul?" Especially during a company event where we went on a sailing trip on the river, paint the bridge from red to gray and it's basically a Bosphorus cruise. Most of the touristy areas resemble the Taksim-Macka-Besiktas district too. The parts descending toward the center are literally Akaretler hill; the vibe is identical.

## What to See

Many people have already made great recommendations, so let me just note the extra highlights we found:

- [Castelo de S. Jorge](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Castelo+de+S.+Jorge+Lisbon+Portugal) - We watched the sunset here and absolutely loved it. We were lucky enough to catch an incredibly long sunset. Since the castle is close to the sea, the Bosphorus-like view was stunning. Highly recommended.
- [Arco da Rua Augusta](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Arco+da+Rua+Augusta+Lisbon+Portugal) - Hands down the most impressive historical structure we saw in the city. Mesmerizing architecture.
- [Igreja de Sao Vicente de Fora](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Igreja+de+Sao+Vicente+de+Fora+Lisbon+Portugal) and [Panteao Nacional](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Panteao+Nacional+Lisbon+Portugal) - Two places we forgot to add to our itinerary. We only noticed them while admiring the view from the castle and could only see them from the outside at night. Photos suggest the interiors are beautiful too; worth a visit.
- [Sintra](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Sintra+Portugal) - Absolutely phenomenal. We did a day trip but in hindsight I'd stay at least one night and spend two full days there. There's so much to see, and spending an evening in that mystical historic atmosphere would be something special. [Quinta da Regaleira](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Quinta+da+Regaleira+Sintra+Portugal) (especially the [Poco Iniciatico](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Poco+Iniciatico+Quinta+da+Regaleira+Sintra+Portugal) inside) and [Parque e Palacio Nacional da Pena](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Palacio+da+Pena+Sintra+Portugal) are the two must-sees. We also managed to visit [Parque e Palacio de Monserrate](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Palacio+de+Monserrate+Sintra+Portugal) - the building itself isn't extraordinary, but the gardens are absolutely worth it, with enormous and gorgeous plants and trees. Uber is widely used and genuinely cheap, so you can comfortably ride between these spots.

## Food & Drink

- **Nata pastries** - Over our 8-day Portugal trip, we probably tried nata from about 10 different places. It's a truly original flavor that never gets old. The best one by far was at [Pasteis de Belem](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Pasteis+de+Belem+Lisbon+Portugal). The place is famous for a reason, don't skip it.
- We stumbled into a wine shop at a market-like spot. So glad we did. They occasionally open incredibly aged Port wines and sell them by the glass. We tasted a Port wine from 1878, normally a 1,000-euro bottle that you'd probably never get to try, for 70 euros a glass. Seventy euros for a single glass is still steep, but being able to do this at all is priceless. If you're a wine lover, tasting something from the 1800s that has survived to the present day is truly a once-in-a-lifetime experience.
- **Ginginha** - A local sour cherry liqueur unique to the area. The flavor is decent, but one place had an amazing presentation: first they pour it into bitter chocolate shot glasses, you do a shot, then another round with a slightly different version, and then you eat the chocolate glasses themselves. They also offer you cherries that have been soaking in ginginha. A unique experience. The place was [Ginginha do Carmo](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Ginginha+do+Carmo+Lisbon+Portugal).

## Tips & Advice

- The airport and train station were calm. Especially the train station, entry and exit are super easy; arriving 10-15 minutes before your train is more than enough.
- City traffic can get jammed occasionally and traffic lights cause extra delays. Whatever Uber or Google Maps shows as the arrival time, always add 10-15 minutes. I never once saw it be accurate.
- For sunset spots, there are many options recommended by others, but the castle worked perfectly for us.

title: Porto
body:
## Overview

Considerably smaller than Lisbon, Porto is a city where three days is more than enough for sightseeing, yet it struck me as a more charming place for long-term living. Many people have already shared great recommendations, so I wanted to focus more on things I think get overlooked or that we especially loved.

## What to See

- The ceramic tile work that dominates all of Portugal initially creates some prejudice because of how we use tiles back home, it almost makes the buildings look like they have bathroom cladding. But especially in this city, the way tiles are used on churches and historical buildings is absolutely stunning. [Chapel of Souls](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Capela+das+Almas+Porto+Portugal), [Church of Saint Ildefonso](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Igreja+de+Santo+Ildefonso+Porto+Portugal), and [Igreja do Carmo](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Igreja+do+Carmo+Porto+Portugal) are some examples, and each one is worth seeing separately.
- [Livraria Lello](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Livraria+Lello+Porto+Portugal) is genuinely very crowded. Getting in is one struggle, walking around is another. If you can manage to visit during uncrowded hours (weekdays, before noon, etc.), I strongly recommend doing so.
- [Mercado Ferreira Borges](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Mercado+Ferreira+Borges+Porto+Portugal) was a lovely find we stumbled upon while walking. Because it was a weekend, there were quite a few open stalls, and the craftsmanship at every single one was outstanding, woodwork, t-shirts, jewelry, phone cases, everything. It was so beautiful we could barely restrain ourselves. Worth checking if the same setup runs on weekdays.
- Watching the sunset from [Luis I Bridge](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Ponte+Dom+Luis+I+Porto+Portugal) on a rain-free day is truly priceless. We originally planned to watch from the hilltop at the end of the bridge as people had recommended, but the view from the bridge itself was so gorgeous that we decided to stay right there. No regrets whatsoever.

## Food & Drink

- I had huge preconceptions about Francesinha, I honestly thought it would turn out to be a knockoff. After eating it at [Cafe Santiago](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Cafe+Santiago+Porto+Portugal), everything changed. It's absolutely not health food, but if I lived in Portugal, I'd demolish one at least once a month. The waiter there also has a little notebook with greetings written in every language. He asks where you're from and immediately responds in your language. Clearly someone who loves his job. Very charming.
- Not Portuguese cuisine, but [Ris8tto da Baixa](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Ris8tto+da+Baixa+Porto+Portugal) is an excellent spot, great value. I normally don't even like risotto and find it absurd because of how similar it is to our pilaf, but this place has transcended the genre.

## Tips & Advice

- For wine tasting, the Gaia side has tons of options. Due to weekend availability and lack of research on our part, we ended up at [Porto Cruz](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Porto+Cruz+Vila+Nova+de+Gaia+Portugal) and left quite disappointed. The port wines they served were pretty mediocre. If this is something you're planning, doing proper research beforehand is absolutely essential.
- Definitely dedicate one full day to the [Douro Valley](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Douro+Valley+Portugal). If you're into wine, I'd strongly recommend staying overnight. As a wine enthusiast, I genuinely regret not spending more time there. I could easily have spent 3-4 days just there without getting bored, there are so many different wineries, and the natural beauty of the setting is magnificent. We used Oporto Road Trips for a tour with the 3-winery option and were very satisfied. They also have 2-winery + boat tour options.

title: Portugal
body:
## Overview

I really enjoyed this country, where I spent 9 days as a tourist. I'd gone in with oddly low expectations. Despite everyone who'd visited before saying great things, my expectations somehow never rose, but by the time I left, I was thinking we could have stayed even longer.

I also explored with an eye toward potentially living abroad as a remote worker, and it seemed like a viable option on that front too.

## Tips & Advice

First and foremost: these people are essentially Turkish. I'd almost say we resemble them more than we do the Greeks. With Greece, our food and drink culture overlaps heavily, but with Portugal, it's our mentality, our experiences, our general country-level problems that are nearly identical.

Their biggest current problem is immigration from abroad. While Turkey's immigration is mostly lower-income, we also have substantial upper-tier immigration (from Russia, Iran, other Arab countries), which has driven up housing prices. Portugal's situation is the same. Golden visas and tax exemptions for foreign freelancers have attracted a flood of Americans who've snapped up all the best central properties. Locals are being pushed further and further out. Sound familiar?

The taxi driver profile is a perfect match with ours too. Despite using Uber, one driver took us somewhere completely different from our stated destination, then asked for an extra 10 euros to take us where we actually wanted to go. Another started complaining the moment we got in about how the fare was too low for this route and this traffic. As if he didn't know the price before accepting the ride. You won't miss Turkey one bit in that department.

In recent years, their other major issue has been people deliberately starting forest fires, then having the burned land handed over for large-scale construction. When I heard this, I genuinely said "no way." Are we sure we're not in Turkey?

Their purchasing power is reportedly similar to Greece's, but while Greeks never seem particularly unhappy about their situation, Portugal is very different. Everyone is genuinely miserable. You can see it's created a sense of inferiority, and like in Turkey, people work incredibly hard, adding a layer of exhaustion. It's genuinely sad to witness.

The football obsession, strong family bonds, our appearance, all virtually identical. Despite everyone speaking English, they'd default to Portuguese with us on first encounter. That's how strong the resemblance is.

And the cherry on top: after late-night drinking, they go out for soup at 4-5 AM. Not tripe soup exactly, but the fact that this shared culture exists is genuinely amazing.

Unlike other European countries, what I loved most here as a Turk was the absence of prejudice. In Germany, the Netherlands, France and similar countries, we unfortunately face veiled racism due to negative perceptions and our population density, nobody can deny this. In Portugal, the Turkish population is genuinely tiny, so there hasn't even been an environment for prejudice to develop. That made it a positive prospect for long-term living. Hopefully we'll never need it.

A fellow reader rightfully pointed out I forgot to mention the chestnut culture. They really do love their chestnuts. They're sold everywhere, and a hotel we stayed at even left some in our room as a welcome gift.

title: Ribera del Duero
body:
## Overview

Spain's premier wine region alongside [Rioja](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Rioja+Wine+Region+Spain). Some of Spain's most premium wines are produced here. The area is so popular with tourists that even on a weekday at noon, winery reservations are fully booked. Visitors come from all over the world solely to taste wine.

What struck us most on this trip was that [Ribera del Duero](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Ribera+del+Duero+Wine+Region+Spain) is essentially [Denizli-Cal](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Cal+Denizli+Turkey) in Turkey. Our main goal was actually to examine this similarity, but we never imagined it would be this exact. It's literally the answer to the question: "What would Cal look like if Turkey weren't a Muslim country, or if Islam didn't prohibit wine?" The difference is that while Cal's wine route is just getting started, maybe 10 wineries total, modest annual tourist numbers, Ribera del Duero has 300 wineries receiving over 300,000 visitors a year. An enormous revenue source for the region.

Turkey probably utilizes at most 10% of its tourism potential. There are so many lessons to learn from countries like Italy and Spain, which share our soil composition and history.

title: Geneva
body:
## Overview

Our second visit to this city. This time we explored with a friend who lives there, taking a more critical eye, but it didn't quite meet our expectations. Probably because our first visit was pure tourist mode, this time the city felt very mixed in terms of its population. Apparently 40% of residents are expats. No different from New York or London, finding a Swiss local in this massive city is tough. Still, nobody stands out negatively; there are no safety issues, and since immigrant integration is handled well, it doesn't create problems.

## What to See

- The most important sights: the [Jet d'Eau](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Jet+d%27Eau+Geneva+Switzerland) fountain and the world's longest bench.
- [CERN](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=CERN+Geneva+Switzerland) is a bit outside the city but absolutely worth the trip. A genuinely enjoyable visit. The only problem is the subject matter is so complex that even the simplest explanation feels complicated. I didn't know the Hadron Collider spans the entire city, it's spread across an enormous area.
- The right bank wasn't impressive for residential architecture, big, blocky apartment buildings. The left bank is beautifully preserved with old historic buildings and neighborhoods.
- The [Carouge](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Carouge+Geneva+Switzerland) district is quite charming, nicely decorated houses. A good 4-5 hour area to spend time in.
- The [Musee d'Art et d'Histoire](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Musee+d%27Art+et+d%27Histoire+Geneva+Switzerland) has a countdown that shows an incredibly long number. If you look it up, it's counting down to when the Sun will collapse into a black hole, the end of our solar system. I have no idea what frame of mind inspired that, but it's quite fascinating.
- The chess park ([Jeux d'Echecs](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Parc+des+Bastions+Chess+Geneva+Switzerland)) was wonderful. We'd seen a similar one in Moscow before. If we were locals, we'd probably be regulars. Even just watching the players is entertaining.
- If you're into wine, the UNESCO-protected [Lavaux](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Lavaux+Vineyard+Terraces+Switzerland) region is 1.5 hours away. A beautiful area for exploring.
- If you have time, Lausanne and Montreux are nearby cities, suitable for day trips.
- Evening trains become less frequent, definitely check schedules and plan ahead.

## Food & Drink

- [Laderach](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Laderach+Geneva+Switzerland) is truly an outstanding chocolatier. Their truffle chocolate is hard to beat anywhere. Swiss chocolate is unquestionably number one in the world. Belgian chocolate can't compete.
- On our previous visit, we caught a medieval festival near the castle. The fully cheese-sauced hot dog we ate at one of the stands still haunts our taste buds. If you encounter something similar at a festival stand, don't miss it.
- [Le Relais de l'Entrecote](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Le+Relais+de+l%27Entrecote+Geneva+Switzerland) is superb. They have a fixed menu of steak and fries. They don't take reservations, which means big queues at opening time. Since the place is large, the line moves fast, but don't leave it too late. The food was excellent, the sauce was phenomenal.

## Tips & Advice

- Uber is considerably more expensive than trams. Using public transport makes much more sense.
- Whether it's selective eating preferences or a new trend, I don't know, but large children are being pushed around in strollers everywhere. Families probably do it for convenience, but I've never seen this many big kids in strollers anywhere before.
- The water is surprisingly hard with high lime content. Locals struggle with their appliances because of it. For a place right in the heart of the Alps, this was quite surprising.
- Geneva is apparently one of the rare places in Switzerland where waste separation isn't mandatory. Being behind on this in the heart of Europe is quite surprising.
- Their Ubers arrive in Range Rovers. That level of wealth.
- The traffic light optimization in the city is zero. We hit 10 red lights in a 5-minute stretch. Not fitting for a city like this.
- Some homes have solar panels even on their balconies. Our friend's apartment is heated by solar energy. That they can harness solar this effectively at such altitude and cold while our country lags so far behind is genuinely saddening.
- One of the rare European cities where urinal partitions actually exist.
- People constantly greet each other. Apparently one of the unwritten rules. Everyone is polite, a lovely aspect of the city.
- The yellow flowers planted around the area are actually potato fields. Production is quite widespread.

title: Bergama
body:
## Overview

The city overall is rundown and has grown in that typical unplanned, aesthetically unfortunate Turkish small-town way. That said, some houses in the city center have been nicely preserved, and there are others under restoration, so it should look even better in the coming years. Still, if this were an Italian city, the whole thing would be pristine, the [Red Basilica](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Kizil+Avlu+Bergama+Turkey) fully restored, and it would be an insanely popular destination drawing 10 million tourists a year. When you consider Turkey's historical attitude toward preservation, you just count your blessings.

The city itself is remarkably clean, almost no litter anywhere. We even saw a man spot bread crumbs on the ground, go fetch a broom, and come back to sweep them up. The locals seem genuinely conscious about keeping things tidy.

## What to See

The Red Basilica in the city is absolutely worth seeing. Even though part of it is in ruins, what remains is still quite stunning. It was apparently a temple for Egyptian gods, all the way out here, which really makes you wonder. Part of it is currently used as a mosque, but it blends in seamlessly.

## Food & Drink

The locally praised cigirtma and Bergama kofte didn't really blow us away. Pretty standard eggplant dish and meatballs. However, I'd definitely recommend stopping by [Salepcioglu](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Salepcioglu+Helva+Bergama+Turkey) for their helva, very tasty and fresh.

title: Küçükköy
body:
## Overview

The town center isn't bad, but the road getting there from [Ayvalik](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Ayvalik+Balikesir+Turkey) is an absolute disaster. There used to be some kind of waste disposal facility, but if you filmed the current state of the road and uploaded it to YouTube labeled as Nepal, nobody would question it. Trash everywhere, plastic bags blowing around, piles of construction debris and rubble. I can't remember seeing anywhere this bad in the Aegean region in a long time.

The town center itself has nicely preserved old houses, and the cafes look pleasant. Worth a 1-2 hour stop if you're passing through. The village's old mosque has quite an original character, and the courtyard features a plane tree where you can sit and enjoy the atmosphere.

Would I go back specifically? Debatable. But as a quick 1-2 hour stop while passing through, [Kucukkoy](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Kucukkoy+Ayvalik+Balikesir+Turkey) is a charming little town. Minus the trash around it.

title: Nysa
body:
## Overview

An ancient city that would be a tourist magnet in any other country. When we visited on a Friday in September, it was completely empty. After scanning our museum cards at the entrance, we asked the attendant if he had any tips for the visit and got a flat "no" in response, which should have been our first clue about how neglected this place is.

## What to See

There's actually quite a bit of active excavation work going on inside, with newly opened areas too. But for an ancient city this large and this well-preserved, both the signage and its popularity are shockingly weak. The location is incredibly accessible, just a 15-minute detour off the Izmir-Aydin motorway. The site is [Nysa Ancient City](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Nysa+Ancient+City+Sultanhisar+Aydin+Turkey).

The theater is beautifully preserved. It held 15,000 people. With a bit of cleanup, they could host concerts here. One of the most intriguing details was the systematic holes in the seating stones. I initially guessed they were for rainwater drainage, but fellow readers corrected me, poles were inserted into them to stretch fabric overhead for shade. Apparently this was typically for nobility.

The city layout is clearly visible. Leaving the theater, you follow a long narrow street directly to the agora, where shops line the left side. Past the agora, you reach what feels like a main boulevard that stretches all the way to the parliament building, itself essentially a miniature theater.

The most impressive part is definitely the tunnels and columns. There's a clear elevation difference between the theater section and the stadium area. They bridged this gap with two-story passages allowing quick transit between the upper and lower sections. The stadium sits in a riverbed, so it hasn't been as well preserved, likely battered by seasonal flooding.

New residential areas are gradually being opened through ongoing excavations. There's a freshly uncovered road where you can still observe the layers of earth that preserved it, layer upon layer of protection visible in cross-section.

A truly enjoyable ancient city overall. I'll happily revisit every 5-6 years to follow its progress. For anyone who hasn't been yet, go as soon as you can.

title: Urla
body:
## Overview

Urla is a vacation town that has become increasingly known for its gastronomy. After taking visiting friends to the restaurants here, I consistently hear feedback that these chef restaurants put even the Michelin-starred places in Istanbul to shame.
